Title: From a 30-Mile Radius to Global Leverage: How Soccer Shots Buffalo Scaled Without Breaking the Bank
Section 1 Content:
Mark runs Soccer Shots in Buffalo, New York. Twenty-six part-time coaches. Hundreds of kids. And the constant challenge every small business owner knows: how do you scale without drowning in payroll?

For years, Mark's hiring strategy was simple. Post a job. Hope someone good applies within driving distance. Settle for whoever shows up.

Then he hired Gustavo, a media and marketing director from Venezuela, through Sagan Passport. The cost? 75% less than a local hire with the same experience.

That one decision didn't just fill a role. It changed how Mark thinks about building his business.
Section 2 Title: LOCAL HIRING WAS KILLING HIS MARGIN
Section 2 Content:
Mark's director of coaching was doing everything: hiring coaches, running training sessions, handling evaluations, putting out fires. His operations director was buried in logistics. Both were stretched so thin that strategic work kept getting pushed to "next quarter."

When Mark looked at hiring a marketing director locally, the numbers didn't work. Someone with real experience would cost $60,000 to $80,000. For a youth sports franchise, that's a massive bet.

"The equivalent starting pay here would've been at least 15 to 20K more," Mark said. "I wouldn't have been able to afford someone with his experience locally."

So Mark did what most small business owners do: he kept doing the marketing himself. Late nights. Weekends. The work that never felt urgent but always mattered.
Section 3 Content:
Mark's advisor Jon asked him a simple question during a planning session: "What if you only did magical things?"

Not the admin work. Not the social media posting. Not the scheduling. Just the high-value work that only Mark could do: building relationships, improving training programs, thinking strategically about growth.

That question led Mark to Sagan Passport. Within weeks, he hired Gustavo.

"I honestly didn't think this was possible," Mark admitted. "But Gustavo's been a game changer for us."

Gustavo took over Soccer Shots' media and marketing. He learned the brand voice, adapted to the business quickly, and started showing up in leadership meetings with ideas. Not as a contractor checking boxes, but as a real team member thinking about the business.

"He's been great, very adaptable," Mark said. "It's only been a month, but he's already learning the language we use and the intricacies of our operations."
Section 4 Content:
With Gustavo handling marketing, Mark's local team could finally focus on what they do best.

His director of coaching stopped scrambling to fill coaching shifts and started improving training standards. His operations director got out of the weeds and into strategic planning. Mark himself had time to think about acquiring new territories and launching innovative programs.

The financial impact was immediate. By spending $15,000 to $20,000 less on the marketing role, Mark could reinvest that capital into growth initiatives. But the bigger win was mental space.

"Now, I can't stop thinking about what else I can outsource globally," Mark said. "Honestly, I've been up at night jotting down ideas. It's addicting in a good way."

Soccer Shots Buffalo went from reactive to strategic. From Mark doing everything to Mark doing the things that actually move the needle.
Section 5 Title: COST SAVINGS ARE JUST THE START
Section 5 Content:
Mark's story isn't just about saving money on payroll. It's about breaking free from the local hiring trap that keeps small businesses stuck.

When you can only hire within a 30-mile radius, you're limited by whoever happens to live nearby and is looking for work. When you hire globally, you're choosing from the best talent available, period.

"Global hiring is more than just cost savings," Mark said. "It's about expanding what's possible."

Today, Soccer Shots Buffalo operates more efficiently. The local team focuses on high-impact work. Gustavo handles marketing and branding. And Mark finally has the capacity to think about what's next instead of just surviving what's now.

"It's not just about saving money," Mark emphasized. "It's about elevating the whole team."
Section 6 Content:
The Hire:

  • Role: Media & Marketing Director

  • Location: Venezuela

  • Cost: 75% less than local equivalent ($15K-20K savings annually)

  • Time to impact: 1 month

The Outcome:

  • Local leadership freed up to focus on coaching quality and operations strategy

  • Marketing function professionalized without inflating overhead

  • Owner capacity unlocked for strategic growth initiatives

  • Mindset shift from "local hustle" to "global leverage"

The Bottom Line: Mark went from doing everything himself to building a team that actually scales. One global hire chang

Title: How Galleon Built a World-Class Remote Team Without the Premium Price Tag
Section 1 Content:

It's a classic disruptive play: take advantage of lower transaction costs that incumbents won't pass along to consumers, build a more efficient operating model, and scale fast.

But to pull that off, Galleon needed to move quickly. They needed AI engineers to build their multi-agent platform. Operations specialists to optimize workflows. Technical talent who could execute without the overhead of traditional Silicon Valley salaries.

Traditional hiring methods were too slow and too expensive. Recruiting agencies wanted $8,000 per hire. Local tech talent in major markets commanded six-figure salaries. The math didn't work for a lean startup trying to disrupt a 112-year-old industry.

That's when Amanda found Sagan.
Section 2 Content:
Galleon had product-market fit. They had a clear vision. What they didn't have was time to waste on slow hiring cycles or budget to burn on inflated salaries.

The challenges were straightforward:

  • Speed: Hiring high-quality talent quickly to match product development timelines

  • Cost: Keeping operations lean while competing against well-funded incumbents

  • Quality: Finding technical and operational talent who could execute in a remote-first environment

"We knew we could build a world-class real estate platform," Amanda said. "But to do that, we needed a high-performance team without the excessive costs of traditional hiring."

Traditional recruiters were expensive and slow. Posting jobs and hoping for the right candidate felt like gambling. And paying Silicon Valley salaries for remote workers who lived in lower-cost markets made zero sense.

Galleon needed a hiring partner who understood their urgency and could deliver quality talent at a price that didn't kill their runway.
Section 3 Content:
Through Sagan Passport, Galleon built a fully operational remote team in weeks, not months.

Here's what that looked like:

Pre-Vetted Global Talent Instead of sorting through hundreds of resumes and conducting first-round screens, Galleon got access to pre-vetted candidates with the technical, operational, and strategic expertise they needed. Sagan's network cut weeks off the sourcing and screening process.

Reduced Hiring Costs Galleon filled critical roles with top-tier global professionals at 60% lower cost than traditional US hiring. No inflated recruiting fees. No overpaying for talent based on zip code.

Accelerated Time to Hire Sagan's process meant Galleon could onboard new team members in days instead of months. When you're building a product that's racing against competitors and market timing, speed matters.

Focus on Product, Not Hiring Logistics By offloading the hiring process to Sagan, Amanda and her team could focus on what actually moved the business forward: building their AI-powered platform, refining their product, and growing their user base.

"Sagan helped us find the right people, fast," Amanda said. "Instead of wasting months on hiring, we were able to build a top-tier team and focus on revolutionizing real estate."
Section 4 Content:
Since partnering with Sagan, Galleon has scaled rapidly while maintaining a capital-efficient operation.

The Impact:

Built a Fully Operational Remote Team Galleon hired and onboarded multiple high-performing team members across operations, engineering, and product in record time. No long hiring cycles. No wasted months searching for the right fit.

Saved 60%+ on Hiring Costs Compared to traditional US salaries and recruiting fees, Galleon cut their hiring costs by more than half. That capital went back into product development and growth initiatives instead of inflated overhead.

Accelerated Product Development With the right people in place, Galleon shipped their Navigator platform faster. Their multi-agent AI system got built. Their transaction workflows got optimized. The team could focus on innovation instead of firefighting staffing gaps.

Enhanced Operational Efficiency New hires didn't just fill seats. They improved processes, automated workflows, and elevated the customer experience. Galleon went from scrappy startup to a professional operation without losing speed.

WHY SAGAN WORKED FOR GALLEON

For startups like Galleon, traditional hiring is a trap. It's slow, expensive, and filled with inefficiencies that kill momentum.

Sagan gave Galleon three things they couldn't get anywhere else:

Speed Faster hiring cycles meant Galleon stayed ahead of competitors. When you're disrupting an industry, timing matters. Sagan cut months off their hiring timeline.

Quality Pre-vetted professionals with the right skills and mindset. No gambling on resumes. No wasting time on candidates who looked good on paper but couldn't execute.

Cost Efficiency 50-70% savings compared to traditional hiring. That's not just margin improvement. That's the difference between running out of runway and having the capital to scale.

Scalability A global talent pipeline for future growth. As Galleon expands, they don't have to rebuild their hiring process from scratch. They have a partner who understands their needs and can deliver.
Section 5 Content:
Amanda talks about a concept she calls "your OPEX is my opportunity." It's the AI-era version of Bezos's "your margin is my opportunity."

Incumbents in real estate are bloated with overhead. They have massive teams. Expensive office leases. Legacy systems that require armies of people to maintain.

Galleon doesn't have that baggage. They built a lean, remote-first operation from day one. They used global talent to keep costs down without sacrificing quality.

And that's their competitive edge.

"If you come from the recruitment industry, you are implicitly disincentivized from seeing global talent as an opportunity," Amanda said. "But if you're an outsider and you just look at basic economics and basic human capabilities, it would be very ignorant to not acknowledge this is an opportunity."

That same principle applies to startups across every industry. The companies that figure out how to operate more efficiently, whether through AI, global talent, or better processes, are the ones that win.
Section 6 Content:
The Challenge:

  • Fast-scaling PropTech startup needed to build a remote team quickly

  • Traditional hiring was too slow and expensive

The Solution:

  • Partnered with Sagan Passport for vetted global talent

  • Hired operations specialists, AI engineers, and technical roles in weeks

The Outcome:

  • Accelerated product development and time to market

  • Fully operational remote team executing at high level

Title: How Remixalot Reclaimed 20+ Hours a Week and Grew Revenue 30% with One Global Hire
Section 1 Content:
Dan Kirner runs Remixalot, a video production and content automation company that serves B2B clients and event organizers. He's got the skills. He's got the clients. And he's drowning in admin work.

Scheduling calls. Following up with clients. Coordinating projects. Quality control. The work that keeps the business running but doesn't actually grow it.

Dan had a team handling the on-the-ground production work, but he was still the bottleneck. Every client email went through him. Every project needed his sign-off. Every schedule change required his attention.

The business worked, but it didn't scale. Dan was trading hours for dollars, and there were only so many hours in the day.

He knew the solution wasn't working harder. It was building leverage. That's when he found Sagan.
Section 2 Content:
Dan's challenge wasn't unique. It's the trap every service business owner falls into.

You start the business because you're good at the work. Video production. Content creation. Whatever it is. But as you grow, the work that made you successful becomes the thing that limits you.

For Dan, that looked like:

  • Spending 15+ hours a week on scheduling, client follow-ups, and project coordination

  • Personally handling every client communication because he didn't trust anyone else to maintain quality

  • Wanting to expand into automation and event signage services but having no capacity to build them

  • Stuck in a consulting model where revenue was capped by his personal availability

Dan wasn't lacking opportunities. He was lacking time.

"I was spending my days managing logistics instead of growing the business," Dan said. "I knew I needed help, but I didn't want to overpay for local talent or deal with the complexity of traditional hiring."
Section 3 Content:
Dan didn't need to hire five people. He needed to hire the right one person and build systems around them.

Through Sagan Passport, Dan hired a skilled virtual assistant who took over the work that was eating his time:

Client Communications and Follow-Ups No more responding to every email personally. Dan's assistant handled initial outreach, follow-ups, and basic client questions. Only the high-value conversations made it to Dan's desk.

Project Coordination Scheduling shoots, coordinating with vendors, tracking deliverables. All the logistics that kept projects moving but didn't require Dan's expertise got delegated.

Quality Control Support Dan's assistant didn't replace his eye for quality, but they handled the administrative side of reviews, feedback loops, and approval processes. Dan could focus on the creative decisions that actually mattered.

The result? Dan reclaimed approximately 15 hours per week. Hours he immediately reinvested into business development, strategy, and building out new service offerings.
Section 4 Content:
With his time freed up, Dan didn't just get his life back. He grew the business.

Time Savings: 20+ Hours Per Week Between hiring a global assistant and implementing automation workflows for video editing, Dan got over 20 hours back every week. That's half a work week that he used to spend on logistics and admin.

Revenue Growth: 30% Increase in Six Months With capacity to focus on business development, Dan closed 30% more new clients within six months. He wasn't stuck in delivery mode anymore. He could actually sell.

Operational Efficiency: 40% Faster Production By combining his global assistant with no-code automation tools for video editing and content repurposing, Dan's team cut production time by 40%. Faster turnaround meant happier clients and more capacity to take on work.

Service Expansion: New Revenue Streams Dan finally had time to build out his event signage automation services. He added real-time dashboard reporting and predictive analytics for ticket sales. These data-driven services gave him a competitive edge and led to a 25% increase in client retention.

"Working with Sagan helped me step out of the day-to-day bottlenecks in my business," Dan said. "With global hiring and automation, I've created leverage that allows me to focus on bigger opportunities while still delivering great results for my clients."
Section 5 Title: YOU DON'T NEED MORE PEOPLE, YOU NEED THE RIGHT PERSON
Section 5 Content:
Dan's story isn't about hiring a massive team. It's about making one strategic hire that unlocks everything else.

Most service business owners think they need to hire locally. They think they need someone in the office. They think the cost of a great assistant is $60,000 a year plus benefits.

Dan hired someone for a fraction of that cost through Sagan. Someone who was just as skilled, just as reliable, and just as committed to the business.

The difference wasn't the person. The difference was breaking free from the belief that good talent has to be expensive and local.

Once Dan had that leverage, everything else became possible. The automation workflows. The new service lines. The 30% revenue growth.

But it all started with one hire that gave him his time back.
Section 6 Content:
The Challenge:

  • Service business owner stuck in day-to-day operations

  • 15+ hours weekly spent on admin, scheduling, client follow-ups

  • No capacity to expand services or focus on growth

  • Revenue capped by personal availability

The Solution:

  • Hired skilled virtual assistant through Sagan Passport

  • Delegated client communications, project coordination, and admin tasks

  • Implemented automation workflows for video production

  • Built systems that didn't rely on founder involvement

The Outcome:

  • 20+ hours reclaimed per week (15 from assistant, additional from automation)

  • 30% revenue growth in six months from new client acquisition

  • 40% faster production turnaround times

  • 25% increase in client retention from expanded service offerings

  • Successfully launched event signage automation services

The Bottom Line: Dan went from being the bottleneck in his business to being the growth driver. One strategic hire through Sagan gave him the leverage to scale without inflating his overhead.

Title: How a $70M Dental DSO Solved Its Biggest Bottleneck: Recruiting Doctors
Section 1 Content:
Dr. Dave Ensley runs Celebrate Dental & Braces, a doctor-owned DSO with 22 locations across multiple states. They're doing $70 million in group revenue. Not bad for a dentist who started out just wanting to straighten teeth.

But here's the thing about growth: the problems change.

When you're one location, the problem is getting patients. When you're five locations, the problem is hiring enough hygienists. When you're 22 locations doing $70 million, the problem is recruiting orthodontists fast enough to keep expanding.

Dave had hit a wall. His biggest constraint wasn't demand. It wasn't capital. It wasn't real estate. It was talent.

Specifically, finding qualified orthodontists and building the operational infrastructure to support them without inflating overhead.

That's when he found Sagan.
Section 2 Content:
Dave's challenge wasn't unique to dentistry. It's the same challenge every service business faces when scaling: how do you find enough specialized talent to support growth?

For Celebrate Dental, that looked like:

Recruiting Bottleneck Finding qualified orthodontists was the number one constraint on expansion. Every new location required a doctor. But orthodontists don't grow on trees. And the traditional recruiting playbook, post on Indeed, wait, hope, wasn't cutting it.

Operational Overload Insurance billing was a nightmare. Patient coordination was falling through the cracks. The front desk person who was supposed to be greeting patients was also chasing down insurance claims and following up on failed payment processing.

Cost Structure Problems Hiring locally for every administrative function was expensive. Dave needed specialized roles, a dedicated recruiter, a call center team, a billing department, but at $50,000-$80,000 per US-based hire, the math didn't work at their current revenue per location.

"We couldn't grow because we weren't doing the job right," Dave said. "But we didn't have the money to hire the right roles because we weren't big enough yet. It's the classic chicken and egg problem."
Section 3 Content:
Dave didn't need to hire one person. He needed to build specialized teams that could operate at scale without killing his margins.

Through Sagan Passport, Celebrate Dental built three core teams:

1. Recruiting Engine

Dave's biggest growth constraint was finding orthodontists. With Sagan's support, Celebrate Dental built a dedicated recruitment function:

Global Recruitment Coordinators A team that manages job postings across LinkedIn, Indeed, and niche orthodontic job boards. They coordinate outreach, engage candidates, and ensure timely follow-ups. No more orthodontists applying and hearing back three days later.

Employer Branding Content The team created targeted content highlighting Celebrate Dental's doctor-owned model and competitive compensation. They built a Facebook group for orthodontists looking to relocate. They got active in LinkedIn groups at the top 20 orthodontic schools.

Referral Program Management They implemented a referral incentive system for current employees, fully managed by the Sagan team. Existing orthodontists could refer colleagues and the team handled all the logistics.

The result? Faster time-to-hire and a consistent pipeline of qualified candidates.

2. Call Center + Revenue Cycle Management

Patient coordination and insurance billing were eating up time at every location.

15-Person Call Center in Mexico Dave hired a manager in Mexico who built a team of 15 representatives handling appointment scheduling, patient inquiries, and payment follow-ups. Call answer rates increased 25%. Patients who called actually got through.

20-Person Billing Team in the Philippines Insurance claims, payment processing, accounts receivable follow-up—all the soul-sucking work that keeps dental practices running but nobody wants to do. This team reduced claim denials and improved collections without adding overhead at each location.

AI-Powered Automation Sagan helped Celebrate Dental implement tools to detect billing issues early and flag accounts needing follow-up. Instead of manually reviewing every claim, the system surfaces the problems that need attention.

3. Finance + Marketing Teams

Senior Accountant in Pakistan A remote accounting team now handles bookkeeping, closes financials faster, and keeps the books clean. No more waiting until the end of the quarter to know if locations are profitable.

Digital Marketing Team Three people managing paid advertising, SEO, and social media. They're generating leads without increasing domestic payroll. The team includes a paid ads specialist with CMO experience, an SEO expert, and a social media manager.
Section 4 Content:
By integrating Sagan's global workforce, Celebrate Dental didn't just save money. They unlocked capacity.

Faster Recruiting Reduced time-to-hire for orthodontists and support staff. The recruiting function went from Dave posting ads on Indeed to a dedicated team running a full recruiting engine.

Better Patient Experience 25% increase in call answer rates. Proactive appointment scheduling. Patients stopped showing up angry that nobody called them about a failed payment.

Operational Efficiency Cost-effective remote teams freed up on-site staff to focus on patient care. The front desk person stopped doing insurance billing and started greeting patients.

Sustainable Growth Through Labor Arbitrage Here's the strategic win: by saving 60% on administrative and operational roles, Celebrate Dental could offer above-market salaries to orthodontists. They became the most attractive place to work in their markets.

"If you paid orthodontists 25% more in starting salary, would you solve your recruiting problem?" Dave asked himself. "Yes. So let's go find that money."

He found it in the call center. He found it in billing. He found it in accounting. He built a more efficient labor structure and redeployed those savings into attracting top-tier clinical talent.
Section 5 Content:
Most businesses think their recruiting problem is about posting better ads or using fancier software.

Dave figured out the real solution: pay more.

But you can't just inflate payroll without killing your margins. So you need to find the money somewhere else.

Celebrate Dental found it by building specialized global teams for everything that didn't require a dental license. Call center. Billing. Accounting. Marketing. Recruiting coordination.

That gave them the financial flexibility to pay orthodontists above market rates without raising prices or crushing profitability.

Now they're the most attractive employer in their markets. And their growth constraint shifted from "we can't find doctors" to "how fast can we open locations?"

"Money talks," Dave said. "We make so much money in Kansas City because we pay well. It's that simple."
Section 6 Content:
The Challenge:

  • $70M doctor-owned DSO across 22 locations

  • Recruiting orthodontists was the primary growth constraint

  • Insurance billing and patient coordination creating operational drag

  • Local hiring too expensive to build specialized teams

The Solution:

  • 15-person call center in Mexico for patient coordination

  • 20-person billing team in the Philippines for RCM

  • Dedicated recruitment team managing outreach and employer branding

  • Finance and marketing teams in Pakistan and globally

  • AI-powered automation for billing and call quality monitoring

The Outcome:

  • Faster time-to-hire for orthodontists and support staff

  • 25% increase in call answer rates

  • Improved collections and reduced insurance claim denials

  • 60% cost savings on administrative roles

  • Redeployed savings into above-market orthodontist salaries

  • Became most attractive employer in target markets

The Bottom Line: Celebrate Dental didn't just save money with global talent. They solved their recruiting bottleneck by building a more efficient labor structure that let them pay clinical staff more than anyone else, without raising prices or killing margins.

Title: How Zen Windows Hired an Installation Manager Who Knew Nothing About Windows, and He Became Their Best Hire
Section 1 Content:
Justin Brown is the Director of Operations at Zen Windows, a window installation franchisor with 24 locations across 11 franchisees.

He had holes all over his org chart. Sales consultant assistants. Installation managers. Back office coordinators. The usual growing pains of a franchise trying to scale.

Traditional hiring wasn't working. Every time Justin posted a job looking for "5+ years window installation experience," he got expensive candidates who topped out fast or cheaper candidates who couldn't handle the complexity.

Then Justin tried something different.

He stopped hiring for window experience and started hiring for something else entirely: smart, hungry, and coachable.

"I want people who love to learn," Justin said. "I can teach them windows in a few days. What I can't teach is tenacity, adaptability, and drive."

That philosophy led him to hire Patrick, a guy from Costa Rica with zero window experience, as an installation manager.

Within 60 days, Patrick had rebuilt their entire warranty process, questioned inefficient systems, and was exceeding every expectation Justin had set.

This is the story of how Zen Windows broke the traditional hiring playbook—and won.
Section 2 Content:
Justin's challenge was the same one every growing franchise faces: how do you find people who can actually do the job, not just people who claim they can?

For Zen Windows, traditional hiring created three problems:

Expensive Hires with Low Ceilings Candidates with 10+ years of window experience commanded high salaries. But Justin kept finding that experience didn't correlate with performance. They knew windows, but they couldn't adapt to new systems. They were set in their ways.

Role Misalignment People were getting hired for one thing and ending up doing another. Skills didn't transfer. The person who interviewed great turned out to be terrible at the actual job.

Hiring Bottlenecks Slowing Growth Every open role meant weeks of posting, screening, interviewing, and hoping. Justin was spending more time managing the hiring process than actually running operations.

"I came from a world where you got thrown in the deep end," Justin said. "I went to school for English and ended up troubleshooting locomotives. I've done event marketing, driven semi trucks cross-country, and now I'm running operations for a window franchise. If I can learn anything, so can anyone else, as long as they've got the drive."
Section 3 Title: HIRE FOR ATTRIBUTES, TRAIN FOR SKILLS
Section 3 Content:
Justin made a philosophical shift. Instead of looking for "window installation experience," he started looking for three things:

  1. Smart - Can they figure things out?

  2. Hungry - Do they want to grow?

  3. Coachable - Will they take feedback and improve?

Through Sagan Passport, Justin found two standout hires who embodied this philosophy:

Ulysses (Nicaragua) - Sales Consultant Assistant

Ulysses wasn't hired for a sales role. He was at a live hiring event for something completely different. But when Justin heard him talk about his love of learning, he knew.

"I asked him: What's the most recent thing you learned that you didn't think you'd ever learn?" Justin said. "He told me he spent hours on YouTube figuring out how a combustion engine works, just because he was curious. He's not even a car guy. That's when I knew."

Justin hired him as a Sales Consultant Assistant at $1,100-$1,200 per month, full-time US business hours.

Within weeks, Ulysses was updating systems, making calls, and learning the sales process. Now Justin is transitioning him into a full sales role.

"If sales doesn't work, I've got a bunch of other things I know he can do," Justin said. "That's the fun part. He's just got unlimited upside."

Patrick (Costa Rica) - Installation Manager

This is the big one.

Patrick had zero background in windows. None. He was originally from the Netherlands, lived in Nicaragua, and had just moved to Costa Rica with his wife.

Justin hired him as an Installation Manager—a critical operational role managing warranties, customer communication, and installation processes.

"I gave him seven days to read the entire playbook," Justin said. "Then on Monday, I threw him in. He looked at me with a straight face and said, 'You got it.' No hesitation."

Patrick spent his first week studying. Then he started executing.

Within 30 days, he was already tackling things Justin had planned for the 90-day mark.

By 60 days, Patrick had:

  • Rebuilt the warranty claims process

  • Questioned inefficient systems and fixed them

  • Reorganized spreadsheets and file structures

  • Turned the warranty function into a "humming machine"

"This guy came in and just said, 'Why is this process like this? I don't like it,'" Justin said. "I told him to fix it. And he did. He had the access, the autonomy, and the drive. That's all you need."
Section 4 Content:
By hiring for attributes instead of experience, Zen Windows saw immediate improvements:

Faster Onboarding Employees were fully functional within weeks. Patrick hit his 90-day goals by day 30. Ulysses was taking sales calls within his first month.

Higher Performance Ceiling Employees hired for attributes kept growing. They didn't top out. They kept asking for more responsibility, more training, more challenge.

Better Role Fit Because Justin wasn't locked into "this person must have X years doing Y," he could move people into roles where they actually thrived. Ulysses might end up in sales, or operations, or somewhere else entirely, wherever his strengths fit best.

Cost Efficiency Without Sacrificing Quality At $1,100-$1,200 per month for full-time US business hours, Justin was getting A-players at a fraction of US salary costs. Not because they were "cheap labor," but because he was accessing a global talent pool and hiring based on potential, not pedigree.

"Patrick turns on a different personality with customers," Justin said. "He's all business with me—straight to the point, no fluff. But on the phone with a customer? He's charming, professional, exactly what we need. That's the kind of adaptability you can't teach."
Section 5 Title: STOP LOOKING FOR EXPERIENCE, START LOOKING FOR DRIVE
Section 5 Content:
Justin's philosophy is simple: Anyone can learn anything if they're smart, hungry, and coachable.

The traditional hiring playbook says: Find someone with 10 years of experience doing exactly what you need them to do.

Justin's playbook says: Find someone who loves to learn, give them a week to study, throw them in the deep end, and see if they swim.

"I keep a running list of things I'm doing that I don't want to do anymore," Justin said. "Then I find someone with the right attributes and train them. If they can't do it, I fire them fast. But if they can? I just keep feeding them more."

That's how you build a team that scales.

Not by finding people who already know everything. But by finding people who are hungry to learn everything.

"I used to focus on industry experience," Justin said. "Now I'm thinking bigger. With the right people, anything is possible."
Section 6 Content:
The Challenge:

  • Growing window installation franchise with 24 locations

  • Traditional hiring focused on industry experience wasn't working

  • Expensive hires topped out quickly or misaligned with actual needs

  • Hiring bottlenecks slowing growth

The Solution:

  • Shifted philosophy to hire for attributes: smart, hungry, coachable

  • Hired Ulysses (Nicaragua) as Sales Consultant Assistant at $1,100/month

  • Hired Patrick (Costa Rica) as Installation Manager with zero window experience

  • Gave new hires autonomy, training, and the space to prove themselves

The Outcome:

  • Patrick rebuilt warranty process and exceeded 90-day goals by day 30

  • Ulysses transitioning into full sales role after excelling in assistant position

  • Faster onboarding (weeks, not months)

  • Higher performance ceilings (employees keep growing instead of topping out)

  • Cost efficiency without sacrificing quality

The Bottom Line: Zen Windows stopped asking "Do you know windows?" and started asking "Do you love to learn?" That one question unlocked a talent pool that traditional hiring would have completely missed.

Title: How One Entrepreneur Stopped Living in His Inbox and Got His Life Back
Section 1 Content:
Neel Parekh runs a growing remote team. Multiple projects. Constant emails. Ideas flying at him from every direction.

He was capturing everything—notes in Apple Notes, tasks in Notion, emails flagged for follow-up. But he was drowning.

His inbox dictated his day. He'd start every morning responding to emails and realize at 3 PM he hadn't done a single thing that actually moved his business forward.

"I was busy all day but never felt productive," Neel said. "I'd finish the day exhausted with a list of things I still needed to do. It felt like I was constantly reacting instead of leading."

Sound familiar?

Neel needed a system. Not another app. Not another productivity hack. A real system that would let him capture everything, prioritize what mattered, and actually execute without losing his mind.

That's when he came to me asking about Getting Things Done (GTD), David Allen's productivity framework that I've been using for years.

We spent a few sessions working through it. And within weeks, Neel went from reactive chaos to strategic clarity.

Here's how.
Section 2 Content:
Neel was doing a lot of things right. He was capturing tasks. He was taking notes. He had tools.

But he had four core problems:

1. Task Capture Without Processing

Neel would dump ideas into Apple Notes or Notion. A URL here. A vague note there. "Talk to Sarah about that thing." But he never went back and actually processed them into actionable next steps.

So his "system" was just a graveyard of half-formed thoughts that he'd never look at again.

2. Everything Felt Urgent

When you don't have a system for prioritizing, everything feels equally important. Emails. Projects. Random ideas. Neel would jump between them all day and never feel like he was making progress on anything.

3. Email Was His To-Do List

His inbox had 500+ emails. Some were actionable. Some weren't. But because he didn't have a better system, he treated his inbox like a task manager.

The result? Constant context switching. Reactive work. Strategic projects getting pushed to "next week" forever.

4. No Structured Review Process

Neel would occasionally look at his task list. But he didn't have a weekly review cadence. So things would slip through the cracks. Projects would stall. He'd forget to follow up on important stuff.

"I knew I was missing things," Neel said. "But I didn't know what I was missing. That's what stressed me out the most."
Section 3 Content:
We walked through the core principles of GTD and adapted them to Neel's workflow. Here's what we implemented:

1. Capture and Process (Not Just Capture)

The Old Way: Neel would see something interesting, save the URL to Apple Notes, and never look at it again.

The New Way: Every time Neel captures something, he immediately asks: "What's the next action?"

  • If it's actionable and takes less than 2 minutes → Do it now

  • If it's actionable and takes longer → Add it to Next Actions list with a clear verb ("Email Sarah about Q1 budget")

  • If it's a multi-step thing → Create a project with a defined end state

  • If it's interesting but not urgent → Move to Someday/Maybe list

No more vague notes. Every captured item gets processed into something clear and actionable.

2. Next Actions, Organized by Context

Neel's task list used to be a giant pile of "stuff to do."

Now it's organized by context:

  • @Computer - Things he needs a laptop for

  • @Phone - Calls to make

  • @Errands - Out-and-about tasks

  • @Waiting - Things he's waiting on from other people

When he's at his desk, he looks at @Computer. When he's in the car, he looks at @Phone. No more scanning through 50 tasks trying to figure out what's actually doable right now.

3. Projects with Clear End States

Anything that requires more than one action is a Project in GTD.

Example:

  • Bad project: "Website redesign"

  • Good project: "New homepage live with updated copy and hero images"

The difference? The second one has a clear end state. You know when it's done.

Neel learned to define projects this way. And suddenly, instead of having 20 vague "projects" floating around, he had 8 clearly defined outcomes he was working toward.

4. Someday/Maybe List (The Relief Valve)

This was huge for Neel.

He had all these ideas and aspirations that weren't urgent but he didn't want to forget. Launch a podcast. Write a book. Explore a new market.

Instead of letting those clutter his active task list, he moved them to a Someday/Maybe list that he reviews weekly.

"That was liberating," Neel said. "I could acknowledge the idea without feeling guilty that I wasn't working on it right now."

5. Email as Inbox, Not To-Do List

Neel adopted the Do, Defer, Delegate, Delete method for email:

  • Do - Takes less than 2 minutes? Handle it now.

  • Defer - Takes longer? Process it into his task system and archive the email.

  • Delegate - Someone else should do this? Forward and track on @Waiting list.

  • Delete - Not actionable? Archive or delete immediately.

He scheduled email processing twice a day—morning and afternoon. No more living in his inbox.

6. Weekly Review (The Secret Weapon)

Every Friday, Neel blocks 90 minutes for his weekly review:

  1. Empty all inboxes - Apple Notes, email, Notion, wherever stuff accumulated

  2. Process everything into the system - Turn vague notes into clear next actions

  3. Review all active projects - Make sure each one has a next action

  4. Check @Waiting list - Follow up on anything that's stalled

  5. Review Someday/Maybe - See if anything is ready to become an active project

  6. Look at calendar for next 2 weeks - Make sure nothing's going to surprise him

This one habit changed everything.

"The weekly review is where I feel back in control," Neel said. "Even if the week was chaotic, by Friday I know exactly where I stand."
Section 4 Content:
• Increased Clarity & Focus: Neel now has a structured approach to processing tasks, reducing mental clutter and uncertainty.

  • More Strategic Work: With clear prioritization, he spends less time reacting to emails and more time on meaningful projects.

  • Reduced Decision Fatigue: Breaking work into actionable steps removes the burden of re-evaluating the same tasks repeatedly.

  • Improved Accountability: The structured weekly review keeps all ongoing projects on track and prevents important tasks from slipping through the cracks.`
Section 5 Content: By implementing the GTD methodology, Neel transformed his workflow from reactive task management to a structured system that optimizes productivity. His ability to capture, categorize, and review tasks effectively ensures he stays focused on high-impact work while reducing stress. Moving forward, Neel plans to refine his system further and explore automation for even greater efficiency.`
Section 6 Content:
The Challenge:

  • Entrepreneur managing growing remote team

  • Drowning in tasks, emails, and random ideas

  • Inbox dictating workflow (reactive instead of strategic)

  • No clear prioritization or review process

  • Decision fatigue from constantly re-evaluating same tasks

The Solution:

  • Implemented GTD (Getting Things Done) framework

  • Captured and processed tasks into clear next actions

  • Organized tasks by context (@Computer, @Phone, @Errands, @Waiting)

  • Created projects with defined end states

  • Moved non-urgent ideas to Someday/Maybe list

  • Applied Do/Defer/Delegate/Delete to email

  • Committed to 90-minute weekly review every Friday

The Outcome:

  • Increased clarity and reduced mental clutter

  • 10+ hours reclaimed per week

  • Shifted from 70% reactive work to 70% strategic work

  • Eliminated decision fatigue through clear next actions

  • Improved accountability with weekly review process

  • No more "Did I forget something?" anxiety

The Bottom Line: Neel didn't need more discipline or longer hours. He needed a system that reduced cognitive load and eliminated constant decision-making. GTD gave him that.

Title: How an Anonymous Twitter Account Became a Profitable Franchise Consulting Firm
Section 1 Content:
Patrick built an audience as Franchise Wolf—an anonymous Twitter account breaking down franchise deals, industry trends, and business opportunities.

He had insights. He had engagement. He had credibility in the franchise space.

What he didn't have was a scalable business model.

His first attempt was a data-driven franchise research platform. Makes sense, right? He had all this knowledge. People wanted franchise information. Sell them research.

Except nobody bought it.

"I learned the hard way that prospective franchise buyers don't want to pay for data," Patrick said. "They want expert guidance. They'll pay a broker or consultant way more money for high-touch advice than they'll pay for a research subscription."

That realization led Patrick to pivot. Instead of selling data, he co-founded FranDogs—a franchise consulting firm that helps people buy, sell, and scale franchise businesses.

But to make it work, he needed leverage. He couldn't just trade his time for money. He needed systems, automation, and the right team.

That's where Sagan came in.
Section 2 Content:
Patrick had the hardest part figured out: distribution.

His Twitter audience was engaged. His content was valuable. Inbound interest was there.

But turning that into a profitable consulting business? That required solving three problems:

1. Content Production Was a Bottleneck

Patrick knew content was his acquisition engine. But producing high-quality YouTube videos, breakdowns, and social content was eating all his time.

He'd spend hours editing footage, tweaking thumbnails, managing uploads. The creative work he loved was buried under production grunt work.

2. Manual Sales and Lead Management

Every inbound lead required personal attention. Qualification calls. CRM updates. Follow-ups. Nurturing sequences.

Patrick was good at closing deals. But he was spending half his time on administrative work that didn't require his expertise.

3. No Systems for Scale

FranDogs was Patrick and his co-founder doing everything manually. Every client interaction was high-touch because they had no automation, no workflows, no leverage.

It worked at 5 clients. It wouldn't work at 50.

"I knew if I wanted to scale FranDogs, I couldn't be the only one producing content and managing the pipeline," Patrick said. "I needed to build leverage into the business model from day one."
Section 3 Content:
Patrick's strategy was simple: use content to generate inbound leads, then build operational leverage to handle them efficiently.

Here's how he did it:

1. Content as the Acquisition Engine

Patrick doubled down on what was already working: B2B content on Twitter and YouTube.

He positioned FranDogs as the go-to resource for franchise consulting. Deep-dive industry breakdowns. Storytelling about franchise deals. Strategic insights that showcased expertise.

The content strategy wasn't about going viral. It was about consistently demonstrating authority and building trust with the right audience.

Inbound leads started flowing.

2. Hired a Video Editor Through Sagan

Patrick hired a video editor for $1,300 per month through Sagan Passport.

But this wasn't just a guy cutting clips. This was an editor who understood storytelling, pacing, and how to turn raw footage into content that actually engaged viewers.

"I didn't want someone who just followed directions," Patrick said. "I wanted someone who could take my rough ideas and make them better. Someone who understood what makes a good YouTube video."

Now Patrick focuses on recording and ideation. The editor handles everything else—cuts, B-roll, thumbnails, uploads. Patrick gets back 10+ hours a week to focus on strategy and client work.

3. Virtual Assistant for Lead Qualification and CRM

Patrick hired a virtual assistant to handle lead qualification, CRM updates, and sales outreach.

Inbound leads get screened. Qualified prospects get routed to Patrick for closing. The CRM stays clean. Follow-ups happen on time.

Patrick still does the high-value sales conversations. But he's not drowning in administrative work.

4. Automation for Lead Nurturing

FranDogs implemented AI-powered workflows for:

  • Automated email sequences that nurture leads over time

  • CRM integration that tracks every franchise prospect

  • Predictive analytics to assess which franchise opportunities are best fits for specific clients

The result? Higher engagement without manual effort. Prospects stay warm even when Patrick's not personally reaching out.
Section 4 Content:
Within the first three months of launching FranDogs with this model, Patrick achieved:

Consistent Inbound Lead Flow

Content continued to drive qualified prospects. No paid ads. No cold outreach. Just high-value content attracting the right audience.

Multiple Franchise Deals Per Month

With operational leverage in place, FranDogs could handle more clients without Patrick working 80-hour weeks. The business became profitable quickly.

Scalable Operations

The combination of global talent and automation meant FranDogs could grow revenue without proportionally increasing Patrick's workload.

High Margins

Consulting businesses traditionally struggle with margins because you're trading time for money. But by building leverage through content, offshore talent, and automation, Patrick kept margins healthy while scaling.

"The video editor alone was a game-changer," Patrick said. "I went from spending 15 hours a week on production to spending 3 hours just recording. That freed me up to focus on what actually grows the business, closing deals and building partnerships."
Section 5 Content:
Patrick's story isn't just about hiring cheaper talent. It's about designing a business model with leverage baked in.

Most consultants start by doing everything themselves. They trade their time for money until they burn out or hit a ceiling.

Patrick did the opposite. He built leverage into FranDogs from the beginning:

Content for Distribution

Instead of relying on paid ads or referrals, Patrick used content to create a consistent inbound pipeline. That's leverage.

Global Talent for Execution

Instead of spending his time on production and admin, Patrick hired a video editor and VA to handle execution. That's leverage.

Automation for Scale

Instead of manually nurturing every lead, Patrick implemented workflows that keep prospects engaged automatically. That's leverage.

The result? A consulting business that can scale without Patrick working proportionally harder.

"The franchise buyers I work with wanted expert guidance, not data," Patrick said. "So I built a business that delivers high-touch expertise, but with operational efficiency that most consulting firms don't have."
Section 6 Content:
The Challenge:

  • Built engaged Twitter audience as Franchise Wolf

  • First attempt (research platform) failed—buyers wanted guidance, not data

  • Needed to monetize content and credibility into scalable business

  • Couldn't trade time for money forever

The Solution:

  • Co-founded FranDogs, a franchise consulting firm

  • Hired video editor through Sagan at $1,300/month

  • Hired virtual assistant for lead qualification and CRM management

  • Implemented automation for lead nurturing and pipeline tracking

  • Continued producing high-value B2B content on Twitter and YouTube

The Outcome:

  • Consistent inbound lead flow from content channels

  • Multiple franchise deals closed per month

  • 10+ hours per week reclaimed from content production

  • Scalable operational model with healthy margins

  • Positioned for sustainable growth without burnout

The Bottom Line: Patrick didn't just start a consulting business. He built a leveraged business model where content drives acquisition, global talent handles execution, and automation keeps the pipeline moving—all while he focuses on closing deals and strategic growth.

Title: How a Plumbing Business Grew Revenue from $6M to $25M in 3 Years
Section 2 Content: Aizik Zimerman acquired a plumbing business and grew it to $25M in three years using global talent. Today, nearly half of his 100-person team came through Sagan. His approach: stop thinking "hiring people" and start thinking "buying business functions." Instead of asking how to keep someone busy 40 hours a week, he asks: "What single thing, done every day, would be worth $18,000 a year to my business?" That mindset let him build entire departments for call handling, recruiting, finance, and quality control without inflating overhead

Title: How an Architect Bought Back Her Business and Rebuilt It Leaner using AI and Sagan as a partner
Section 2 Content: Cathryn Lavery sold her e-commerce business to a PE firm. They laid off her team and changed the strategy. She bought it back and decided to run it differently. Through Sagan, she built a small core team with AI tools integrated from day one. She hired three people from South America: a video editor, an operations coordinator, and a customer success manager. Her approach: hire smart, hungry, coachable people who can grow with the company instead of expensive mid-level US hires who plateau. The result: a leaner business that moves faster and costs less to operate.

Title: How Former Pro Baseball Player Brandon Beachy Scaled His Koala Insulation Franchise and Upgraded Staff Quality with Sagan Talent
Section 2 Content: Brandon Beachy, a former professional baseball player turned Koala Insulation franchise owner in Nashville and franchise consultant, found himself in a bind when his local receptionist quit suddenly. He turned to Sagan and replaced that single employee with two full-time global team members for less than the previous hire's cost. One handles phone calls and scheduling from Argentina. The other, based in Colombia, manages takeoffs for single-family homes and large commercial projects, and is plugged directly into the sales reps' CRM to ensure estimates go out fast and follow-up never falls through the cracks. Brandon plans to use Sagan University's training tracks to develop his global hires into junior leaders.

Title: How Incline Homes Owner Jacob Klein Scaled His Real Estate Businesses and Affordable Luxury Product Using Sagan Talent
Section 2 Content: Jacob Kline runs Incline Homes, a new construction company delivering a "luxury product" at bottom-10% pricing, and One Properties, a real estate acquisition business. He's spent five years building with global talent, using it to scale and access specialized labor at lower revenue thresholds. Through Sagan, he's hired roles like a controller from Pakistan starting at $1,200 a month. His longest-tenured global hire, an assistant from the Philippines, has grown into a key operational role. Jacob actively uses Sagan University training tracks to develop leadership and management skills across his team, and the conversation digs into how the behaviors that helped you survive as a founder can eventually hold your organization back.
